data.title: Intel's Latest Chips Push Innovation Forward
data.description:
Given some of the challenges Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) has faced recently, there’s been an even larger-than-normal interest in the company’s next-generation chips: codenamed Panther Lake for PCs and Clearwater Forest for servers. Not only are they to be the first manufactured with Intel Foundry’s latest 18A process, but they also come at a time when much of the tech industry and even semiconductor industry’s attention has been focused away from Intel and onto companies like Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (because of their strength in GPUs for AI acceleration). That, in turn, led to questions about whether or not Intel could regain its status as a critical player in the chip industry.

Based on the specs and early performance numbers the company just unveiled for the two new families of chips, Intel made it emphatically clear that they and their products (and manufacturing technology) are still a significant force with which to be reckoned. The new Panther Lake mobile SOC (system on chip) - officially called Core Ultra 3 - in particular, builds on the architectural, technological, and power efficiency enhancements the company first made with its most recent Core Ultra 2 processors (codenamed Lunar Lake) and takes these improvements even further. At the same time, Intel was able to address some of the structural limitations of Lunar Lake - including limited configuration choices for CPU and GPU and the fixed amount of on-chip system memory - so that its PC OEM customers now have much more flexibility in designing and configuring systems powered by Core Ultra 3. Plus, based on initial numbers provided by Intel, the new series can leverage many of the performance improvements from Core Ultra 200 series chips (codenamed Arrow Lake). Finally, it’s also interesting to note that Intel is building the software tools necessary to make Core Ultra 3 an important new option for the budding robotics and edge market, so it’s good to see the company’s looking ahead as well.

The new Clearwater Forest server part - which Intel is calling Xeon 6+ - also builds on previous designs and adds key architectural enhancements. Notably, both the Xeon 6+ and the Core Ultra 3 take advantage of the company’s latest efficiency-oriented E-core design, codenamed Darkmont. This design includes large increases in cache sizes and numerous in-depth architectural improvements, both of which contribute to a 17% larger number of instructions per clock (IPC) that it can handle. In the case of the Xeon 6+, which Intel said will be brought to market in mid-2026, the chip only includes E-cores, but there are up to 288 of them per socket, twice as many as in the previous Intel Xeon 6. (As with previous generation Xeon product families, Intel will likely introduce a new series of server processors with the latest performance-oriented P-cores, but nothing has been announced just yet.)

On the Core Ultra 3, which is scheduled to ship later this year, one of the biggest improvements is three different base configurations that include different numbers of CPU and GPU cores. The baseline model features 8 CPU cores (4 each of the E-cores and P-cores), while the other two offer 16 cores (8 E-cores, 4 additional lower-power E-cores, and 8 P-cores). One of the two 16 CPU core models includes 4 of the newly enhanced Xe3 GPU cores (offering up to 50% better performance than the previous Xe2 GPU, according to Intel) and is expected to be paired with an external GPU (Nvidia or AMD) for things like gaming PCs. The other includes 12 Xe3 GPU cores for more all-around graphics performance in applications like content creation tools. The baseline 8 CPU configuration incorporates 4 Xe3 GPU cores.

All the new Core Ultra 3 chips also include an enhanced NPU chip (NPU5) for AI acceleration tasks. At 50 TOPS, the new NPU only offers a modest increase over Intel’s previous 48 TOPS design in Core Ultra 2, but the company emphasized the new design takes up significantly less space and is more power-efficient. Key to better AI performance is faster access to larger amounts of memory, and Intel addressed both of those issues in Core Ultra 3 as well. First, the company removed the memory from the on-die package (as it was in Lunar Lake/Core Ultra 2) and allowed OEMs to put up to either 128 GB of DDR5 SO-DIMMs or 96 GB of LPDDR5 memory in their designs. In addition, the company improved and tiered up the memory access speeds, ranging from 6400 MT/S (Mega Transfers per Second) on the low-end config with SO-DIMMS up to 9600 MT/S with LPDDR5 on the 16 CPU/12 GPU option.

Finally, on the manufacturing front, both the Core Ultra 3 and the Xeon 6+ incorporate several unique Intel Foundry capabilities. First, as mentioned, each of them includes elements - specifically the CPU cores - built on Intel’s own 18A process technology. Not only are the individual transistors smaller on the 18A process than previous manufacturing nodes (think of the company’s previous lead process technology Intel 3 as essentially 30A), but they also incorporate two key technologies - Ribbon FET and PowerVia - that allow them to be more efficient and performant than previous designs. Ribbon FET is Intel’s version of Gate All Around technology, which improves the current flow versus previous FinFET designs, and PowerVia lets power be distributed to the backside of a transistor, enabling more power-efficient designs.

To be clear, the entire Core Ultra 3 and Xeon 6+ are not built in 18A, only portions of it. In fact, some of the elements in both chips are still built with third-party foundries. But equally important to the new process technology is that both chips benefit from Intel Foundry’s leading chip-packaging technologies. These packaging techniques enable complex chiplet designs that incorporate multiple elements (such as the CPU, GPU and base tiles) to be put together in a single SOC package. In particular, these two chips leverage the company’s Foveros chip-stacking technology as well as high-speed EMIB connections. The key takeaway is that these manufacturing technologies both play a key part in driving the kind of performance and efficiency improvements in Intel’s latest chips and provide a solid example of the kind of manufacturing advantages Intel Foundry could provide to other third-party chipmakers.

Ultimately, what these announcements demonstrate is that Intel is not only able to execute on important technology advancements in both chip design and manufacturing, but that the company is listening to the demands of its customers and making the kinds of adjustments it needs to make to stay competitive. Despite their maturity, both the PC market and the server market continue to see new entrants, and for Intel to stay in any kind of leadership role, it needs to deliver the kind of capabilities that both Core Ultra 3 and Xeon 6+ look to offer.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gold Market Commentary: Stick, Twist Or Double Down?
data.description:
New highs…but is there more uplift?

Record monthly ETF inflows took gold to its 39th new high for the year, finishing the month at US$3,825/oz (+12%). Y-t-d gold is up 47%, marking the highest return in a calendar year since 1979 (Table 1).

Our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) suggests political tension, strong options market activity, and currency weakness played a key role in gold’s performance last month (Chart 1). The only drag came from some rebalancing and profit-taking - captured in a gold price lag in the model and reflected in the intraday price dip on 30 September, which was quickly bought.

Gold ETF flows recorded their strongest month on record. Net inflows of US$17.3bn (146t) were dominated by North America (US$10.6bn) and Europe (US$4.4bn); Asia joined the rally (US$2.1bn) and other regions reported modest inflows. COMEX managed money net longs participated in gold’s upward price action, adding US$9bn (+33t).

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

*Data to 30 September 2025. Our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) is a multiple regression model of monthly gold price returns, which we group into four key thematic driver categories of gold’s performance: economic expansion, risk & uncertainty, opportunity cost, and momentum. These themes capture motives behind gold demand; most importantly, investment demand, which is considered the marginal driver of gold price returns in the short run. The ‘residual’ represents the percentage change in the gold price that is not explained by factors already included. Results shown here are based on analysis covering a five-year estimation period using monthly data. Alternative estimation periods and data frequencies are available on Goldhub.com.

Table 1: Gold reached 13 new all-time highs in September and the momentum rolls into October

Gold price and performance in key currencies*

USD(oz) EUR(oz) JPY(g) GBP(oz) CAD(oz) CHF(oz) INR(10g) RMB(g) TRY(oz) AUD(oz)
September price* 3,825 3,260 18,190 2,845 5,325 3,046 114,761 872 159,088 5,785
September return* 11.6% 11.1% 12.2% 12.0% 13.0% 11.0% 12.5% 11.5% 12.8% 10.3%
Y-t-d return* 46.6% 29.4% 37.9% 36.5% 41.9% 28.7% 51.2% 41.6% 72.5% 37.2%
Record high price* 3,827 3,263 18,281 2,849 5,326 3,050 114,761 872 159,107 5,819
Record high date* 29 Sep 2025 29 Sep 2025 29 Sep 2025 29 Sep 2025 30 Sep 2025 29 Sep 2025 30 Sep 2025 30 Sep 2025 29 Sep 2025 29 Sep 2025

*As of 30 September 2025. Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD, expressed in local currencies, except for India and China where the MCX Gold Price PM and Shanghai Gold Benchmark PM are used, respectively. Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

Equities on edge, again

  • October is known for large equity sell-offs, and risks are riding quite high
  • Gold is generally a good hedge, but there may be concerns about its ability to respond given how stretched it looks
  • In addition, a very oversold US dollar continues to pose a threat to gold, should a squeeze materialise
  • However, our analysis suggests that these concerns are not warranted and that, absent a liquidity crunch, gold’s hedging credentials remain intact.

September is, on average, the worst calendar month for US stocks and October is known for big corrections, making this a generally nervous time for equity investors.

The likely threat to equities is acute, given lofty valuations, goldilocks earnings projections, high market concentration, extended positioning, and technical red flags.1

Gold is a great long-run diversifier and a good short-run hedge against equity drawdowns. But because gold is not a contractual hedge,2 the good performance during equity corrections isn’t guaranteed.

What does gold have left in the tank?

There is perhaps a concern that gold - and in the short run this means gold investors - might not respond so readily to an equity lurch, primarily because it looks overbought.

And there is probably some merit to this. Chart 2 shows that from a tactical perspective gold might struggle to find marginal investment buyers in this scenario, even as long-run strategic positioning remains light (Chart 3).

*Data to 30 September 2025. Investment = bar and coin, global gold ETFs, COMEX non-commercial net long. CB = central banks. LBMA gold price PM.

*Data to 30 June 2025. Private investment includes bar and coin, ETFs and estimated OTC positions. Global assets include global equity and bond market capitalisation excluding central bank holdings ex gold.

An additional concern perhaps is that other factors might conspire against gold’s performance: oversold rates or dollar, over-egged fears, and so on.

We looked at a set of these drivers during historical equity drawdowns, addressing questions such as: how does gold fare unconditionally? (Chart 4); and what if gold has already performed really well (vs 200dma), is showing extended positioning, or if the dollar is cheap or oversold at the start of the equity sell-off? Do credit spreads need to blow out for gold to do well? Table 2 shows these factors grouped by initial conditions and contemporaneous changes.

*Data from 31 December 1971 to 25 September 2025. LBMA gold price PM.

What we found was that initial conditions are not great arbiters of gold’s performance during these sell-offs. The only factor that really seems to matter is where the US dollar goes (Chart 5), and to a lesser extent where it sits in valuation terms prior to the sell-off.

*See chart 4 for data range.

The trajectory of yields only matters if we include the volatile mid-1970s and early 1980s observations, while the level of spreads only matters if we exclude the volatile 1970s.

So, where is the dollar? Well, the bad news is that it looks almost as tactically oversold as gold is overbought; it has been dragging anchor for some time (Chart 6), which is unsurprising given the consistent dollar/gold long-run negative correlation.

*Data to 26 September 2025. Net longs represent NYMEX dollar contract positioning in tonnes equivalent.

The good news is that during two-thirds of the equity sell-offs the dollar has fallen and has not been materially influenced by its own initial conditions. To boot, the dollar has been oversold for quite some time, and there is no guarantee that a bounce will materialise just because equities drop.

In summary

Looking outside these factors, central banks have shown a propensity to buy dips over the last three years. And so, it seems, have other investors. A mini-intraday gold selloff on 30 September quickly reversed into the close. So even at these levels, there appear to be investors waiting in the wings. And there are plenty of reasons for investors to be looking at gold. Among these are:

  • US government frictions, including the shutdown in early October
  • Trade tensions not abating
  • Flailing employment as inflation fears linger
  • Dollar-hedging applying continuous pressure on one of gold’s key drivers.

While our analysis is only indicative, it leaves us confident that gold will hold its ground and perhaps see further uplift should equities experience a correction, given the plethora of supportive factors elsewhere. Perhaps only a major liquidity squeeze could upend both gold and equities, but there are no clear signs of fractures in credit or banking sectors…yet.

Table 2: Factors that might affect how gold responds when equities drop from a great height*

*Analysis conducted on daily data from 31 December 1971 to 25 September 2025. Drawdowns measured as window when the S&P 500 index falls from a high to more than 10% below the high.** Steepness measured as S&P 500 return / duration. Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

Footnotes

1Lofty US stock market valuations bank on earnings strength | Reuters

2Hedges that arise from formal contracts with a counterparty to protect against financial risk such as forwards, futures, swaps

Original Post

data.title: Novo Nordisk's Akero Deal: Panic Buy Or Wise Decision? Here's My Take
data.description:
Investment Overview - Context To Today's Deal—Novo Nordisk's Disastrous 2025

At the beginning of this year, I acquired some stock in Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), the Danish Pharma giant. The company had delivered astonishing growth over a four-year period—in September 2020 the stock traded at ~$35 per share and in September 2024 it reached an all-time high value of ~$140, up ~300%.

The gains were underpinned by the Pharma's development of semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) that helps patients' bodies produce more insulin, reducing the amount of glucose produced by the liver and slowing down food digestion by mimicking the effects of the hormone incretin.

The drug is associated with weight loss in patients of ~15% to over 20% (at its highest dose) and was first approved to treat type 2 diabetes as Ozempic in December 2017 and then to treat obesity as Wegovy in June 2021. Further approvals in cardiovascular risk reduction, chronic kidney disease ("CKD") and nonalcoholic steatohepatitis ("NASH"), or metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis ("MASH) have followed.

Wegovy/Ozempic earned $24.9bn of revenues in 2025, the only prescription drug to earn more was Merck & Co., Inc.'s (MRK) cancer med Keytruda with ~$30bn. In February, guidance for 2025 was for sales growth of 16-24%, with operating profit growth of 19-27%, so ~$49bn of revenues and $22bn of operating profit at the midpoints.

Shares had retreated to ~$90 per share after a next-generation GLP drug, Cagrisema, had failed to live up to expectations, but I reasoned that, with one challenger in T2D/weight loss markets that analysts believed would soon be worth over $100bn—Eli Lilly's tirzepatide—Novo stock would continue to thrive.

As I covered in my September note on Novo, the opposite happened. The Trump administration's imposition of tariffs on international exports and earnings miss in Q1, blamed on companies selling compounded versions of semaglutide, which led to the ousting of its CEO. Then came downgraded guidance shared in July, and most recently, another guidance downgrade and a "company-wide transformation to simplify its organisation". This resulted in ~9k job losses, and President Trump's threat to impose a most favored nation ("MFN") and to impose tariffs specifically on pharma imports into the US—Novo's biggest market—has all conspired to drive Novo's share price to under $60 per share at the time of writing—down over 30% year-to-date.

How Novo's Akero Buyout Confounded My Expectations

News broke earlier today that Novo will acquire the California-based biotech company Akero Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:AKRO)—according to a press release:

Under the terms of the agreement, Novo Nordisk will acquire all outstanding shares of Akero’s common stock at a price of 54 USD per share in cash (or an aggregated value of 4.7 billion USD) at closing. In addition, Akero shareholders will receive a non-transferable CVR entitling holders to a potential additional payment of 6 USD per share in cash (or aggregated value of 0.5 billion USD) upon US regulatory approval of EFX for the treatment of compensated cirrhosis due to MASH.

The transaction has been unanimously approved by Akero’s Board of Directors and is expected to close around the turn of the year, upon satisfaction of customary closing conditions, including approvals by regulatory authorities.

EFX refers to Akero's only drug candidate of note, efruxifermin—according to the same press release:

Akero’s fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) analogue efruxifermin ("EFX") is a potentially best-in-class treatment for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH). EFX is currently in phase 3 development for the treatment of patients with moderate to advanced liver fibrosis (F2-F3) and patients with cirrhosis (F4).

EFX is currently being evaluated as a once-weekly subcutaneous injection in the phase 3 SYNCHRONY programme, which consists of three clinical trials designed to support regulatory approval for the treatment of pre-cirrhotic (F2-F3) MASH and compensated cirrhosis (F4) due to MASH.

The phase 3 programme builds on two 96-week phase 2b trials, in which EFX has been observed to significantly improve liver fibrosis and reverse compensated cirrhosis due to MASH. Over 96 weeks, the HARMONY (F2-F3) and SYMMETRY (F4) trial demonstrated 49% and 29% reduction in fibrosis without worsening of MASH, respectively, compared to 19% and 11% in the respective placebo groups1. EFX is the only treatment to have shown significant fibrosis regression in F4 patients in a phase 2 trial.

I covered Akero in a note for Seeking Alpha back in October 2023, after the company's stock crashed from $50 per share to ~$11.50 per share—a loss of over 75%—after the market gave the thumbs down to a 36-week analysis from its 96-week Phase 2B Symmetry study.

I wrote:

The headline news - at least as far as the market is concerned - is that the study missed its primary endpoint, failing to show a statistically significant difference between either the high dose, 50mg, or low dose, 25mg arms and placebo. The data showed that, according to an Akero press release:

22% (28mg EFX) and 24% (50mg EFX) of patients experienced at least a one-stage improvement in liver fibrosis with no worsening of NASH by week 36, compared to 14% for placebo

Nevertheless, I gave Akero stock a Buy recommendation, based on the fact that an earlier study, Harmony, had met its primary goal for both the 50mg and 28mg EFX dose groups, with 41% and 39% of EFX-treated patients, respectively, seeing at least a one-stage improvement in liver fibrosis, and met additional secondary endpoints.

When those results were announced, Harmony stock leaped from $11 per share to over $50 per share, reversing the results of the Symmetry data readout. In August this year, however, with Akero stock trading at $49 per share, I reversed my Buy call to a Sell call, despite the biotech having shared positive 96-week data from Symmetry, revealing that:

Among patients with baseline and week 96 biopsies, 39% of the 50mg EFX group (p=0.009) demonstrated ≥1 stage improvement in fibrosis with no worsening of MASH, representing a 24% effect size over placebo at 15%

By ITT analysis, with all missing week 96 biopsies treated as failures, 29% of the 50mg EFX group (p=0.031) demonstrated ≥1 stage improvement in fibrosis with no worsening of MASH, representing a 17% effect size over placebo at 12%

I noted the level of competition in MASH, quoting from Akero's 2024 annual report / 10K submission as follows:

We understand that a number of pharmaceutical companies, including AstraZeneca PLC (AZN)/MedImmune LLC, Boehringer Ingelheim AG, Eisai, Inc., Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), GSK plc (GSK), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Takeda Pharmaceutical Company Limited (TAK), as well as large and small biotechnology companies such as Aligos Therapeutics, Inc. (ALGS), Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY), Altimmune, Inc. (ALT), Amgen, Inc. (AMGN), Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ARWR), Boston Pharmaceuticals, Inc., Cirius Therapeutics, Inc., Corcept Therapeutics Incorporated (CORT), Inc., D&D Pharmatech, Inc., Eccogene Co., Ltd., 89bio, Inc. (ETNB), Galectin Therapeutics Inc. (GALT), Galmed Pharmaceuticals, Ltd., Hanmi Pharmaceutical Company, Ltd., HighTide Therapeutics, Inc., Inventiva Pharma SA, Ionis Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (IONS), Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, Inc., MediciNova, Inc., NeuroBo Pharmaceuticals, Inc., North Sea Pharmaceuticals, OrsoBio, Inc., Poxel SA, Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (REGN), Sagimet Biosciences, Inc. (SGMT), Sciwind Biosciences Co., Ltd., Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (TERN), Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (VKTX). and Zhejiang Doer Biologics Co. Ltd. are or may be pursuing the development or marketing of pharmaceuticals that target MASH.

I concluded that:

Akero stock itself dropped >$40 in May, before rumours that the company was exploring a sale of the business revived it.

So far, those rumours have proven unfounded - with so many other companies also participating in the "MASH dash" for approval, would a Pharma pay $4bn today to acquire Akero, with its single advanced stage drug candidate, taking on the might of semaglutide, tirzepatide, et al?

I am not sure I see that happening, and I also don't see Akero filing for approval of EFX much sooner than late 2027, which is a long time to expect Wall Street to twiddle its thumbs and wait. Meanwhile, a host of companies will likely showcase promising MASH data.

I was, of course, wrong because Novo has paid $4.7bn for Akero, potentially rising to $5.2bn, but I am quite shocked that the company is Novo, as it already has a drug approved to treat MASH, semaglutide. Novo says the transaction is "not expected to impact Novo Nordisk’s previously communicated operating profit outlook for 2025", but is this the right time for the company to be buying a Phase 3 stage asset not certain to secure approval in a field with over 40 such candidates?

Novo Buys Akero - Rationale For Deal

In September, Swiss-based Pharma Roche Holding AG (RHHBY) announced it would complete the acquisition of the biotech 89bio, Inc. (ETNB) for:

$14.50 per share in cash, plus a non-tradeable CVR to receive certain milestone payments of up to an aggregate of US$6.00 per share in cash representing a total equity value of approximately US$2.4 billion at closing and representing a total deal value of up to US$3.5 billion.

89Bio's lead candidate is pegozafermin and it's an FGF21 analog, just like efruxifermin—the two companies have been developing their drugs in parallel for many years. It's been reported that it was actually Akero's data that persuaded Roche to complete its deal for 89Bio, although apparently, five other interested parties ultimately passed on the opportunity to bid for the company.

While Roche paid a 79% premium to 89Bio's last traded price before the deal was announced, Novo's deal represents a 19% premium to Akero's last traded price—did Novo get a good deal, or should Akero shareholders be celebrating today?

There is no doubt that MASH is a market of genuine significance - prior to the advent of GLP-1 drugs, the "MASH Dash" - the race to secure a first approval to treat the liver disease—was one of the most hyped sectors in drug development. The dash was ultimately won by Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (MDGL) and its drug Rezdiffra, which was approved in March last year and has earned $330m in 1H25.

By semaglutide standards this is relatively peanuts, given Wegovy earned $3bn in revenues in Q2 alone and Ozempic, $5bn, so what has persuaded Novo to part with up to $5.2bn for an asset that won't read out its interim Phase 3 Synchrony Phase 3 study data until mid-2026 and its final data until mid-2027?

Ultimately it comes to treatment of cirrhosis, a more advanced stage of MASH that semaglutide, and Rezdiffra, may ultimately struggle to treat.

As we can see above (slide taken from a recent corporate presentation), efruxifermin may have an advantage in that data suggests it can reverse cirrhosis, something that semaglutide, and most other MASH targeting candidates have not been able to achieve. It's been estimated that the cirrhosis treatment market will be worth ~$1.49bn in 2025, which could rise to $2.15bn by 2032. Analysts have speculated that efruxifermin could achieve peak revenues of $5.8bn by 2037.

If true (and analysts peak revenue forecasts should be taken with a pinch of salt based on my experience), the rationale for today's deal seems clear - not only could Novo potentially look to combine EFX and Wegovy/Ozempic, as a monotherapy, the drug may easily achieve "blockbuster" (over $1bn revenues per annum) within a few years treating cirrhosis patients.

Does This Deal Enhance The Value Of NVO? Is There Any Benefit In Buying AKRO Stock Now?

As mentioned, I was skeptical that a Pharma would part with $4bn to buy Akero in my last note on the company, but Novo has taken the plunge and paid significantly more than that to gain access to EFX.

Novo stock is down -1.4% in trading so far today, suggesting the market is unmoved by the deal, however I have long expressed the view that semaglutide is a once-in-a-generation drug (make that twice in a generation, given Eli Lilly's tirzepatide, or Mounjaro/Zepbound is as effective a GLP-1 drug as semaglutide) that will not only dominate the T2D and obesity landscape for years to come, but is proving its worth in multiple other indications as well, and that therefore, we should expect the company to overcome its "annus horribilis" in 2025 to date, and begin to grow its share price and valuation again.

Given that semaglutide drove ~$8.2bn of Novo's ~$12bn of revenues in Q2, it's important that Novo diversifies away from near-total reliance on its "miracle" drug, so in that sense, the Akero deal makes sense, although there are many hurdles still to be overcome before the drug can be submitted for approval - a far larger Phase 3 study involving ~1.6k patients for example, where the Symmetry study involved 182 patients with F4, compensated cirrhosis.

Novo may have been moved to make the deal to ensure that another Pharma did not gain access to EFX first, but the Pharma could end up with egg on its face if EFX does not succeed in its larger study.

Meanwhile, Akero stock trades at a value of $54, up over 15% in trading so far today, so investors buying stock today could still benefit from the additional $6 per share in Contingent Value Rights, and realize a +10% gain, if EFX makes it to market.

Ultimately, I believe that - if not quite a "panic buy", Novo made today's deal to ensure it remains in control of the future of the emergent GLP-1 market, potentially combining EFX and semaglutide for best-in-class results in certain subsectors such as MASH, and on the premise that EFX has great potential in the treatment of cirrhosis.

It is a calculated gamble, with the near-term risk that the Phase 3 undermines the value of EFX, and while I wouldn't say I remain bullish on Novo despite today's deal, I am unsure whether it will prove a wise decision long-term. I wouldn't buy Akero stock today for the CVRs either, because of the risks involved in succeeding in a larger pivotal study, but I do believe that this deal ensures that EFX ends up in the right hands in terms of its development and in terms of potentially funding a commercial launch.

I'd call this a deal that suits both parties, but not one that fundamentally changes the value proposition for either company - I'm still long Novo Nordisk, and I think Akero management got what they wanted in terms of securing a lucrative exit for shareholders that was not always certain.

data.title: AT&T: Don't Be Tempted To Buy This Dip Now
data.description:
AT&T: Outperformance Over Peers Is Notable, But Could It Have Peaked?

More than seven months on, I think it's timely for me to take a look at how AT&T (NYSE:T) stock has performed since I cautioned that T could be forming a top and urged investors to be wary of entering too aggressively. There have been several notable developments going on in the telco industry recently, with former PayPal CEO Dan Schulman stepping up to take on the CEO role at AT&T's archrival, Verizon (VZ). That has also received media scrutiny recently, as Schulman's comments suggest VZ could bring back past scares of a market share tussle with AT&T and T-Mobile (TMUS).

As a result, it has led to a wave of selling in AT&T and TMUS stock, joining the folks at Verizon, intimating the market's displeasure at such attempts to rock the boat again, as competitive dynamics have somewhat stabilized. T stock's outperformance this year over its most ardent domestic rivals demonstrates the market's confidence in AT&T's convergence strategy while also keeping a tight lid on forward CapEx investment discipline, affording investors the confidence they need in its free cash flow visibility.

Back in my February update on AT&T, I noted that the stock's gains appeared to have reached an exuberant phase. My caution is predicated on a relatively tepid growth profile, suggesting that the outperformance plausibly emanated from a mean-reversion play, benefiting holders who held through the pain trade previously.

I believe AT&T has carefully capitalized on its solid free cash flow business to level up its competitiveness against T-Mobile in the spectrum licenses expansion with the recent deal with EchoStar (SATS), worth $23 billion. I think these are opportunities that shouldn't be missed when presented, allowing the company to close the gap against its rivals quickly, buttressing its edge across previous deficiencies that allowed TMUS to pull ahead. Notably, AT&T management has assured the market that they still expect the CapEx cadence to normalize through 2030, underpinning capital allocation discipline. These are crucial guideposts that I believe investors will take into careful consideration. T's appeal with income investors requires capital allocation discipline to lower the execution risks of unanticipated dividend cuts that could scupper its recovery.

AT&T: Lower CapEx Growth Affords More Leeway For Management

Wall Street is aligned with management's clarifications on the cadence of its CapEx over the next two years, suggesting ongoing normalization while keeping overall spend at a relatively high level. And with topline growth still expected to remain at the low-single-digit mark for the next two years, I believe investors will be carefully watching whether the risks of another market share battle royale between AT&T and its telco peers could be shaping up, which could introduce higher execution risks into their P&L variability.

It's remarkable that AT&T's debt profile has been trending down, augmenting the company's capabilities to double down on its core business while strengthening its partnerships on the direct-to-device front with AST SpaceMobile (ASTS). I think it has afforded the market more confidence that AT&T could manage to strike accretive deals with its robust FCF while keeping leverage ratios at reasonable levels over the next couple of years, in tune with the relatively high CapEx needs through the rest of the decade.

Verizon's recent deal with ASTS has rekindled the competitive dynamics escalating in this space, even though I believe AT&T's deep and widespread core fiber and wireless business shouldn't face material risks of being displaced in the near term. The current technology isn't expected to be robust enough in terms of scale and latency to replace these terrestrial networks. Hence, I view the inroads made by VZ as appearing to be more defensive in nature, as the lagging incumbent seeks to deepen its forays into adjacent markets to improve its possibilities, given its relative underperformance observed in its shares as compared with T stock.

Buy Or Sell T Stock?

When I consider T's valuation relative to its long-term averages, it does appear that the market is seemingly indecisive in pushing beyond the 7.6x forward EBITDA zones, which also aligns with the +1 standard deviation over its 10-year average.

I noted that T's price action has reached oversold levels after the recent selloff, which might stir dip buyers into action. However, given the plausibility for more intense competition emanating from a revived Verizon under the new boss and also the high investment needs through the rest of the decade, AT&T buyers might face challenges in justifying a more aggressive valuation upgrade.

While a bearish rating isn't apt, I urge investors to allow the market more time to allow the stock to show signs of constructive consolidation above the $26 level, scrutinizing for buying support from dip buyers. T's forward yield of 4.3% also doesn't seem that attractive as compared to what income investors could obtain from treasuries, with the 2-year (US2Y) still printing close to 3.6%.

Rating: Maintain "Hold."

data.title: PepsiCo Q3: EPS Beat Due To Price Increases, Not Increasing Volumes (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

PepsiCo, Inc. (NASDAQ:PEP) closed the third quarter with results slightly above consensus, but volumes are down and the beat came from price increases, not revenue growth. The guidance remains the same as in the previous quarter, i.e., low single-digit organic growth and flat core EPS at constant exchange rates, with a cash return of $8.6 billion, coming from $7.6 billion in dividends and $1 billion in buybacks.

I am updating my rating on the stock. I was waiting for this quarterly report for confirmation and to understand the direction, and if I was previously on “hold” awaiting new catalysts (with a nod to the downgrade), I now confirm my “sell” thesis. I see limited upside (if any) and a significant downside, and it is still too early to talk about a serious rerating. There are better options out there, and I prefer to avoid the risk of a “value trap”.

How are we doing?

Pepsi's recent history over the last few months/years was definitely a rollercoaster ride, as you can see from the next chart.

Years ago, the company operated with a hybrid model, owning some of its bottlers but still relying on independent operators for local distribution, including PBG and PAS, two of the largest, with distribution contracts in regions such as the United States, Canada, and Mexico. PepsiCo, the parent company, was a major shareholder in both companies with about 40-43% of the shares, but in 2009, the company made a decision to buy out the rest of the shares for a total consideration of around $7.8 billion.

Perhaps I am going too far back in time, but the reason will become clear shortly. After the acquisition, the operations of PBG and PAS were integrated into a single structure that we now know as Pepsi Beverages North America (PBNA). Let's fast forward to today. The operating margins of the main PBNA division were higher before the acquisition.

In practice, the operating margins of the beverage division have almost halved, from 21.5% in 2009 to 11.2% today, which is no small matter. This is because bottling is a low-margin business, very capital-intensive, logistically and transport-intensive, but not very profitable. Coca-Cola has divested most of its bottling network, maintaining much higher margins over time (23.3% in 2024). This is clear evidence that ‘bringing part of the business in-house’ is not always a good idea, and if PepsiCo decides to separate its bottling business (or spin it off), it is likely that the margins of the beverage division would return to levels comparable with other big players in the sector, such as Coca-Cola.

I am highlighting this because in September, Elliott Investment Management acquired a $4 billion stake in PepsiCo and is now calling for a strategic review, which could include the possibility of spinning off the beverage business from the food business to unlock value. Ultimately, Elliott is an activist fund, and what matters to them is bringing value to the stock, nothing more. I discussed this in an article in September entitled “PepsiCo: Elliott's $4B Bet Promises A 50% Rerating—But I'm Staying Hold,” in which I pointed out that the activist saw a 50% rerating potential in Pepsi but that, in my opinion, that number remained on paper until concrete results were seen. I emphasized loudly that I am not buying Pepsi just because Elliot did. It is interesting to note that large investors are approaching the company, but for now, these are just rumors, and I prefer to buy based on facts.

Earning Results

I saw the numbers and participated as an “auditor” in the conference call.

The reported earnings slightly beat analysts' consensus, and in fact, the stock rose as much as 2% in pre-market trading. With the start of the conference, we were back to even. All in all, therefore, a modest beat, but the picture remains unchanged from my previous view.

Organic growth was 1.3% with volumes down 3%, a sign that inflation (which management itself was concerned about in previous quarters) continues to dampen demand for snacks and beverages in the US, while pricing is still doing the “dirty and heavy” work.

In terms of products/segments, PFNA recorded organic growth of -3% (with volumes down 4%, partially offset by pricing) and PBNA up 2%. The real boost came from abroad, with EMEA up +5.5%, LatAm +4%, and Asia Pacific +1%, so we can say what is earned outside the United States serves to offset domestic losses, not to reignite the group's growth, and this is not good.

Guidance remains unchanged for the whole of 2025, i.e., “low-single-digit” for organic revenue and core EPS at constant exchange rates more or less in line with last year, with $8.6 billion in returns to shareholders (divided between $7.6 billion in dividends + $1 billion in buybacks).

CEO Laguarta says that among the company's priorities are accelerating the innovation pipeline, refining the price-pack architecture to deliver more value (this includes the expansion of brand values such as Santitas/Chester's, which you mention) and “right-sizing” the cost structure. In short, he is saying that there may be more “entry price” products and a more aggressive “cut” in SG&A to finance growth.

On the governance front, a new CFO, Steve Schmitt (ex Walmart), will take his post on November 10. I think he is an expert in operations, skilled in complex chains and cost discipline — a potential ally if savings need to be made.

My interpretation is that the beat here is a result of the price, not a real acceleration in volumes. The erosion of demand, combined with input costs, tariffs, and M&A expenses, indicates that in order to achieve a substantially unchanged core EPS and, above all, to satisfy Elliott's pressure, more aggressive moves are needed in product innovation and cost containment (portfolio simplification, SG&A, distribution network). Therefore, the results beat expectations, but it does not change the narrative (soft US demand, compressible margins).

Valuation

Right now the stock is trading at 16.5x the expected earnings with a discount of nearly -17% to its peers, as you can see in the next table below. Over the last 12 months, it has been trading at 14.5%, so the difference has increased by around 260 bps, which in statistical terms means it is half a standard dev below the average, that it is not an extreme level by itself, but it does signal that the market is still factoring in low growth and margins being less than expected as we saw earlier. Just to compare, Coca-Cola is trading at 21x with a premium over its peers while Monster and Celsius are at even higher multiples.

It is worth noting that the stock has declined by approximately 8% since the beginning of the year, and the quarterly results released today only serve to confirm this trend as well as the precision of the market's valuation. The company, however, exceeded the earnings expectations mainly due to higher prices, as sales volumes in America were weak, and the company got some "help" (in terms of growth) from the rest of the world.

The next table (Sales per Quarter) makes it very clear how the discount has been set up. The PepsiCo row tracks the move from the boom in 2022-H1'23 (double-digit growth mainly by pricing) to 2024 normalization (0/negative) and then stabilization this year: +1.0% in Q2 and +2.7% in Q3. Actually, the price cycle has come back and, without a return of volumes in North America, the top line is still going to be +1–3%. "Growth" brands (energy drinks) on the other hand are still at double digits, Coca-Cola is going at a slower but more stable rate, and some breweries/spirits are in the red.

On the other hand, PepsiCo is keeping a payout of about 65-70% and increased its dividend by 5% on June 30. With a five-year track record, the company has raised its dividend at a rate of approximately 7% per year. This is why I believe the stock should be viewed more as a "bond proxy" and nothing more. The discount could persist for a long time if a more aggressive cost-cutting program (and a slight increase in volume) isn't implemented.

Risks and opportunities

We have already discussed this, but for the sake of accuracy, I will list them again. Among the risks, I see the issue of price, because, as I said earlier in this quarterly report, the beat was “price-led,” so if price leverage slows down (more promotions, trading down) while US volumes remain negative on PFNA/PBNA, we will have a serious problem. Furthermore, as long as bottling remains “in-house,” beverage margins have a structural ceiling, so if no concrete moves are made in the coming months (refranchising, rationalization), the market will continue to price PEP as a low-growth defensive stock, maintaining the discount on multiples. To complete the picture of “problems,” volatile raw materials/packaging, possible tariffs, and customer concentration (Walmart is the largest customer) create a risky mix, meaning that it would take very little to erode the gross margin.

On the upside, if clear productivity/SG&A targets are set, this could boost expected margins and close part of the discount, which would be the quickest catalyst for a re-rating.

Conclusions

I would summarize my conclusion like this: Earnings beat because of price increases, but demand is still weak. PepsiCo trading at 16–17x expected earnings and with a yield of 4% is at the moment a decent bond proxy, but that's about it. From here, the potential gain is quite limited and the risk of loss is not small if prices return to normal and volumes continue to decline. To be safe, I am not taking a position in the stock which might be a defensive value trap: good coupon flow, little operational traction.

data.title: SMH: AI Mania In Full Swing - Peaking Volumes Imply Potential Implosion (Downgrade)
data.description:
SMH's Rally Overly Done, As The Market Enters An AI Bubble

I previously covered the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) in June 2025, discussing how the semiconductor sector had reported a massive reversal after the worst of the market-wide meltdown in April 2025 and the market's overblown fears surrounding the potentially slowing AI spending cadence.

With the ETF's top three holdings delivering double-beat performances and raised future guidance, I had believed that the multi-year cloud spending trends remained robust, aided by the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US/China, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating then.

SMH 1Y ETF Price

Since then, the bulls have continued to defend SMH's upward momentum by +29.6% compared to the wider market at +11.8%, thanks to the multi-year AI super cycle as reiterated by numerous hyperscalers in the Q2'25 earnings season and the weeks after that.

For reference, five of the world's largest hyperscalers by market share, Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), Alibaba (BABA), and Oracle (ORCL) in respective order, have been reporting robust cloud-related revenues while hinting at higher capex trends in 2026.

Many other players, including numerous neoclouds and start-ups, have also highlighted the voracious appetite for computing power while reporting growing deals/accretive acquisitions/increased fundings, with the immense rally also underscoring why "OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has reportedly said that he believes AI could be in a bubble."

The two AI market leaders, Nvidia (NVDA) and OpenAI, have further fed the FOMO rally, with the GPU giant committing a $100B investment in the AI SaaS company contingent on the deployment of at least 10 GW of Nvidia systems, and H1'26 expected to commence the first rollout of the Vera Rubin platform.

The same has been observed in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), with the Lisa Su-led company issuing OpenAI a warrant of approximately 160M shares, or the equivalent of 10% of the former's equity, and the latter expected to deploy 6 GW of AMD's systems moving forward.

This is on top of OpenAI supposedly committing to a >$10B of orders with Broadcom (AVGO), with it naturally buoying the market's conviction surrounding the growing demand for custom AI accelerator chips while lending further credence to the IDTechEx's projection of the overall AI chips demand growing at an accelerated CAGR of +14% to $453B by 2030.

These developments underscore the highly durable (and extremely hefty) AI-related spending trends for the next few years, with it naturally exemplifying the robust prospects of these semiconductor companies along with the world's global foundry, namely, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM).

My optimism for the latter, arises from the fact that TSM commands a growing global foundry share at 70.2% in Q2'25 (+2.6 points QoQ/+7.9 YoY) and the foundry 2.0 share at 35% in Q1'25 (inline QoQ/+0.9 points YoY), with the undisputed market leader set to enjoy robust top/bottom-line performance as the appetite for AI chips proves insatiable.

This is significantly aided by the foundry's ongoing manufacturing expansion globally across Taiwan, Arizona in the US, Japan, and Germany, allowing them to increasingly grow its production capacity, top/bottom-line performance, and consequently, market share moving forward.

These reasons are also why SMH has been able to enjoy a rich +73.1% price gain since the April 2025 bottom, since NVDA, TSM, and AVGO, being the three largest holdings comprising 36.24% of the ETF, have reported immense rallies by +79.3%, +107.9%, and +108% over the same time period, respectively.

Thanks to the growing AI chip demand and the higher foundry capacity utilization, ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) (OTCPK:ASMLF), the world's most valuable lithography company, is likely to consistently report higher net lithography sales and net service/field option sales as well, with it underscoring its highly strategic razor and blade business model.

This is especially since experts have highlighted how data center GPUs may only "survive for 1 to 2 years, or up to 3 years," with the "significant stress" on chips potentially triggering an intensified replacement cycle and, subsequently, accelerated lithography upgrades/servicing cadence.

Another of SMH's holdings, Micron Technology (MU), has proven itself as a leader in the memory market as well, thanks to its close collaboration with NVDA and the growing hyperscaler wins with six market leaders, which has contributed to the growing HBM3E/HBM4 market share and the richer top/bottom lines.

Combined with the end of the prior memory inventory correction in FY2023 and the potentially sold-out position through the end of 2026, it is apparent that MU has been able to capitalize on the ongoing AI boom.

This is significantly aided by the ongoing recovery in the automotive and industrial end markets, with the ADAS/AI-enhanced capabilities already triggering an increased adoption of higher-performance memory solutions and the improved pricing mix contributing to the recovering profit margins nearer to pre-pandemic levels.

A similar cadence has been reported by QUALCOMM (NASDAQ:QCOM) (NEOE:QCOM:CA) as well, as observed in the extremely rich auto/IoT YTD revenues growth profile of +34% YoY and the company seemingly on track to achieving the auto/IoT revenues of $22B in FY2029, expanding at a CAGR of +21.4% from FY2024 levels of $8.33B.

Combined with QCOM's expanding AI use cases on the smartphone platform, along with the diversification to the CPU/smart wearables end markets, it goes without saying that the wider semiconductor market has been well buoyed by the ongoing AI boom and the recovering industrial/auto/IoT end markets.

So, Is SMH ETF A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SMH 5Y ETF Price

These reasons may also be why SMH has hit new peaks of $348s by the time of writing while running away from the 50/100/200-day averages, with it apparent that the AI mania has reached fever pitch levels as FOMO traders/investors also feed the ongoing rally nearing the Q3'25 earnings season.

SMH Valuations

As a result of the immense rally, it is unsurprising that SMH, at a P/E of 43.67x, along with other semiconductor-focused ETFs, appears to be on the expensive side, compared to the wider market at 34.84x.

This is especially since the ETF's top three holdings:

  • NVDA at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.21x compared to the 5Y mean of 47.78x,
  • TSM at 30.69x/22.54x,
  • AVGO at 49.79x/23.41x, respectively,

They are rather inflated while offering interested investors a reduced margin of safety.

SMH's Overbought Position

SMH's technical indicators also suggest its overbought position, as observed in the peaking Relative Strength Index (RSI) and the trading volumes hitting historical peaks, with it remaining to be seen how long the upward momentum may last, depending on the holdings' results in the upcoming earnings calls.

While I have personally invested in many of the ETF's holdings and in the ETF itself, I believe that the investors' buying power are likely to be exhausted soon, and a pullback is rather likely, with it presenting risks to its near-term investment thesis.

As a result, I prefer to prudently downgrade SMH to a Hold here while urging interested investors to accumulate capital and wait for a deep retracement to occur before adding afterwards for an improved margin of safety.

Pending that speculative event, let's wait on the sidelines for now.

data.title: SoundHound AI: Once-In-A-Generation Tech Platform Still In Its Infancy
data.description:
Sometimes, the idea of a certain technological paradigm shift is so inevitable that resisting it seems almost futile. When tech solutions offer utility that drives undeniable cost efficiencies, entire industries are forced to adapt. One such megatrend that's very clearly playing out is the agentic AI transformation, and SoundHound AI (NASDAQ:SOUN) seems to be right at the center of it.

This is my fourth coverage of SoundHound AI, and I'm pretty happy with how my predictions of the stock's performance have turned out so far:

As can be seen above, I have called for strong buy and buy positions on the stock at $10, $13, and $14. Today, SoundHound AI trades at a price of ~$19, and, as I will argue in this assessment, I still feel that the value proposition is intact and arguably even stronger than before.

The company recently announced the deployment of its Amelia 7 agentic AI platform with Apivia Courtage, a major European insurer, marking another major step in its expansion into enterprise AI. The move demonstrates the real-world impact of SoundHound's tech in improving productivity and customer experience in diverse industries.

In this article, I break down why this development validates SoundHound’s long-term strategy and, more importantly, reinforces the case for continued shareholder value creation.

As such, I'm maintaining my buy stance on SoundHound AI.

SoundHound Expands Agentic AI Partnership with Apivia Courtage

Yesterday, SoundHound AI announced a collaborative partnership with the French wholesale insurance broker, Apivia Courtage. Apivia Courtage is a subsidiary of the AEMA Group, which is also among the world’s largest mutual insurance organizations.

As per the announcement, Apivia Courtage will be deploying SoundHound AI’s Amelia 7 agentic AI platform throughout its various contact centers. As such, the Amelia platform will be in charge of handling a wide range of customer interactions, which include identity verification, answering contract-related questions, updating personal information, calculating financial impacts on insurance policies, and scheduling meetings with human advisors.

This collaboration actually builds on an older partnership between the two companies, going back to 2023, which saw Apivia report a 20% boost in its contact center productivity with the help of earlier SoundHound tool deployments. The newly announced deployment extends earlier agentic capabilities by introducing fully agentic AI agents, which are capable of reasoning, planning, and executing complex multi-intent tasks without human escalation.

The newly deployed platform will operate across multiple channels, such as voice, chat, and text, each of which leverages SoundHound's advanced speech recognition and natural language understanding to ensure seamless and more "human" interactions. This setup allows for a lower labor burden while allowing human teams to focus more on higher-value tasks (such as relationship management, etc.).

Strategic Implications for SoundHound

While on the surface there's a lot to appreciate about the advantages the collaboration brings to both parties, I think there's quite clearly a deeper and more strategic narrative about SoundHound AI that's being shaped and is easy to overlook. This relates to the company's leadership positioning in agentic AI as a foundational enterprise platform with a near dominance of the voice AI dimension.

Considering that Amelia 7 is seeing an expanded deployment in an industry as highly regulated and compliance-sensitive as insurance, I believe it seems very clear that SoundHound AI is actually very capable of owning the layer of AI that enterprises of multiple industries can potentially turn to for mission-critical, multi-channel, agentic interactions. This is especially true in cases where there is a need to reason, act, and integrate across human workflows at scale.

With this arrangement, SoundHound AI has successfully demonstrated enterprise trust as well as compliance capability, which will surely prove as a future proof point for other regulated and high-value industries, such as banking, healthcare, and energy. Similarly, the company has also managed to visibly showcase impressive ROI, where organizations report double-digit productivity boosts, making such an investment necessary from a competitive sense. The benefits would directly flow into company margins by significantly driving down operational costs per customer engagement (while minimizing error rates).

What I find most promising, however, is that every SoundHound AI deployment actually reinforces the company's platform moat through network effects. What this means is each implementation generates proprietary interaction data and hones its agentic reasoning capabilities to a higher standard than before.

Artificial intelligence, by its very nature, improves the quality of its output with exposure to real-world data and complex scenarios. Every deployment allows SoundHound AI to capture more nuances in language, better decipher customer intents, and gain a technical edge in industry-specific workflows that cannot be fully replicated in a lab. This proprietary data then pretty much feeds back into the platform, which enhances accuracy, reasoning, and contextual understanding of Amelia 7 for future clients in related or even in different industries.

In essence, through these wins, SoundHound AI is actually widening the gap between itself as the first meaningful mover in the space and potentially new entrants that may look to disrupt. It's doing so by accumulating proprietary data, refining its agentic reasoning, and deepening industry-specific expertise. This is a compounding advantage that makes SOUN stock the perfect long-term growth vehicle that many are seeking out in the tech and in the AI space.

Valuation Assessment

At its current price of almost $19, SoundHound AI holds a market capitalization of $7.4 billion. To some, this might seem like a stretch, considering that the company reports a trailing twelve-month revenue figure of only $131 million, without even turning in a profit at its current stage. This puts the stock's PS ratio at ~57, which is up from the 45x from my coverage in August.

When a stock is trading at 57x its revenue, the market is clearly expecting the company to deliver value that's a whole order of magnitude higher than it's currently reeling in. In SoundHound's case, I see this as being easily justifiable, as the company seems quite able to successfully scale its agentic AI platform across multiple large enterprise clients and replicate the productivity gains seen at Apivia Courtage. This playbook makes the company very valuable, allowing it to unlock massive TAM in high-value industries like banking, healthcare, and telecommunications, which are in addition to the automotive and service industries it's already focused upon.

I believe that investors should not be distracted by the $131 million revenue figure and instead consider that its total backlog amounts to about $1.2 billion (which makes its current $7.4 billion market cap more palatable). In fact, what I'm most compelled by is the actual scale of the TAM the company is targeting and how quickly it could realistically capture it. The management estimates the size of this opportunity to be at around $140 billion, at the very least. For context, SoundHound's revenue currently touches <0.1% of that estimation. Capturing 1% of this market would see the company's revenue increase 10x. I feel that the company is capable of capturing a meaningful chunk (40%-plus) of this market in the upcoming years, given its clear technical edge, its proven track record of enterprise deployments across different industries, and the meaningful ROI it delivers to clients.

I see SOUN as a phenomenal growth stock that's likely to compound through the next few years into the most dominant player in the enterprise agentic AI space.

As such, I reiterate my buy rating on the stock.

Risk to Thesis

While the agentic arena is not exactly crowded right now, from a competitive sense, I feel that big tech will eventually notice the opportunity in voice AI and AI-driven customer engagement at scale. Right now, while it may seem that SoundHound is going to own this layer of the AI stack, the largest tech companies out there, whether Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), or even Apple (AAPL), could possibly pursue a path that involves bundling voice AI and agentic capabilities into products and platforms. Unlike SoundHound, these players have massive distribution channels that would enable them to reach hundreds of millions of users across the world instantly.

This is a risk that investors need to keep in mind because it could cut short the company's lofty ambitions, which would bring the stock's high multiples back down to earth.

This being said, however, I would not be too paranoid about this risk because SoundHound AI does hold the advantage of specialized expertise and deep integrations. The company has institutional knowledge built up over 20 years, which cannot exactly be replicated overnight.

Investor Takeaway

SoundHound AI is a perfect example of a company positioned at the heart of an inevitable technological inflection. Its strategy of embedding its platforms within enterprise ecosystems ensures both resilience and compounding learning effects. Moving forward, as the agentic layer becomes a necessity rather than an option, I strongly believe that SoundHound’s AI platform stands to benefit from the network effects of adoption and data.

data.title: S&P Global: AI Analyzes, But Humans Decide (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
AI Enthusiasm Does Not Apply To Every Company

I often hear talk about a potential AI bubble, but even amid this enthusiasm, you can still find solid, undervalued businesses. On one hand, AI is beneficial for all those companies that invest heavily in cloud computing/automation, but on the other hand, it questions all those companies with a traditional business model.

In particular, market data firms are going through a complex period since FactSet downwardly revised its revenue guidance for the fiscal year. As I explained in a previous article, I think the market reaction was totally irrational; after all, subscriptions and revenue are still experiencing a positive growth rate. Of course, lower growth than anticipated, but I don’t think this justifies a drop comparable to what happened in the middle of the Great Financial Crisis.

Where does all this pessimism come from?

The market narrative is that AI will gradually replace market data firms, something that I believe is very unlikely. If anything, AI can enhance their platforms, not replace them.

In this general panic that began with FactSet (FDS), even exceptional companies such as S&P Global Inc. (NYSE:SPGI) are being dragged down, falling about 15% from its all-time high.

As you can see from this chart, such a drop doesn’t happen very often, and every time it has occurred, the stock usually recovered pretty fast: a great company can’t stay cheap for long.

When the market eventually recognizes that AI can’t replace this financial giant, but it will be a tool to enhance it, then it will probably be too late to buy SPGI.

Why AI Can’t Replace SPGI

At present, I believe there is excessive enthusiasm surrounding AI, as if it were going to wipe out millions and millions of jobs in the coming years. I don't want to downplay recent technological advances; I just don't believe that human judgment no longer carries any weight.

Let’s take, for example, the SPGI rating segment, responsible for 30% of revenue and 47% of operating income.

AI has been a hot topic for years now, yet I see a constant revenue growth and a very high profitability (large improvements over the last decade). AI should already have replaced part of this business, but in my opinion, this is not happening for two reasons.

The first one is that we are still far from fully trusting AI with such an important issue. Credit rating needs human judgment, years of experience, and developed interpretation skills. AI-based rating would rely just on objective components, without involving any critical thinking ability. Here is an example.

Let’s assume that we need to assess a rating for a company that has been struggling for the past three years, but it has just announced a new CEO. Since the latter is not quantitative data, it is complicated for an AI model to figure out how important this information is. Therefore, the AI-based rating might not properly weigh a relevant component.

This is just an example, but I think you got the point. AI struggles to elaborate on qualitative data; that’s why human judgment is still the best choice.

The second reason is just a matter of reputation. S&P Global, alongside Fitch and Moody’s, has been dominating the rating industry for decades, and has accumulated a tremendous amount of experience in everything related to it. It has thousands and thousands of trusted clients, and it has succeeded in overcoming every recession and regulatory issue. To become a credit rating company, it is required to be identified as a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization, a long and complex process to deal with.

A new AI model cannot even be compared to any of these giants, simply because no one would rely just on a machine for their investment decisions. We are humans, and we only trust other humans when it comes to making important decisions.

Rather than replacing market data companies, I think AI can potentially be a growth driver. Automating operating tasks, speeding up data interpretation, are all pros to further improve the profitability of this industry (already quite high).

Let’s now take a look at the Indices business segment, responsible for 12% of total revenue and 20% of total operating income.

Here as well, despite the AI hype going on for years, there is no sign of growth slowing down. The reason is pretty simple: the human judgment wins once again.

S&P Global indices are used worldwide as a benchmark by most analysts; the S&P 500 index is the most important one to evaluate stock market performance. After decades of dominance in this industry, it is unlikely, to say the least, that AI-based indices will replace those we are all familiar with.

In this industry, there is no necessity for this kind of innovation, which is why I am rather confident that SPGI indexes will keep providing increasing revenue over the long term.

Finally, let's look at the Market Intelligence segment, responsible for 32% of total revenue and 16% of total operating income.

Compared to previous segments, this one doesn’t present the same profitability, but it remains AI replacement-proof.

The Market Intelligence segment includes multiple businesses, from firm consulting to offering solutions for asset managers/investment bankers, as well as providing an investing desktop platform called S&P Capital IQ. Overall, it is a wide-ranging segment and makes critical reasoning its strong point.

Over the decades, it has established a bond of trust between S&P Global and its clients, which is why I don’t think the latter will prefer a non-empathetic smart chatbot to consultants that have been helping them for years.

In this case as well, I don’t see how AI can be considered an imminent problem for this business segment. At best, it can become a useful tool at the consultant's disposal. After all, it's a bit like the medical field: if you're sick, do you trust AI's opinion more than your doctor's?

Conclusion

I analyzed the segments responsible for 83% of SPGI’s operating income, and frankly, I am not worried at all about the current market narrative. I don’t think this is the end of market data firms; on the contrary, AI might be a further growth catalyst.

This might be the right time to add some shares of SPGI, or more in general, some shares of these kinds of companies.

According to the Price/Free Cash Flow multiple, its current valuation seems quite cheap compared to the historical average, which is why I think a buy rating is deserved. So this means an upward rating compared to my last article on SPGI. Back then, the stock was traded at $535, so it was worth the wait.

I can’t know in advance if this is the bottom, but I genuinely don’t care: the more it drops, the more I buy. This is what I always do when it comes to investing in great businesses. In my opinion, SPGI has the potential to be a permanent company in my portfolio, so a buy-and-hold strategy is definitely the most appropriate.

However, I didn’t buy any shares yet because of what is happening to FactSet right now. Basically, I've invested so much in the latter that I can't do anything else before the end of the month.

The stock is priced as if we were in the middle of a financial crisis, but the GDP is growing more than 3%, and FactSet’s revenue and users are both growing. If you want to know more about it, I suggest you read this article.

data.title: The Market Loves Rollins. Maybe Too Much.
data.description:
Rollins, Inc. (NYSE:ROL) is one of the best businesses we can find in this market. It is a stable, boring business that is growing at a high pace and showing little fluctuations. However, in the past, I always considered the stock to be a little too expensive for being a good investment and in my last article, which was published at the beginning of this year, I rated the stock a “Hold” once again. In the conclusion of my last article I wrote:

In the end, we should still be very cautious about an investment in Rollins. We could make the case for Rollins being fairly valued, but always have to keep in mind that high-growth rates are necessary. Rollins is certainly not a bargain and although the business is recession-resilient and will most likely continue to grow at a high pace, it will remain on my watch list for now.

But it seems like the market disagreed with me and although the stock was already trading for 50 times earnings, it climbed from $46 back then to almost $60 right now and an investment in the stock would have generated a return of 28% (and therefore clearly outperformed the S&P 500, which increased by only 16%).

In the following article, I will look at Rollins once again and try to answer the question of how much of a premium a wide economic moat and stable performance might be worth and at what price I would consider buying Rollins. But as always, we start by looking at the last quarterly results.

Quarterly Results

In July 2025, Rollins reported its second quarter results and while earnings per share were in line with expectations, the company did beat revenue estimates. Year-over-year, revenue increased from $891.9 million in Q2/24 to $999.5 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 12.1% year-over-year growth. Operating income also grew 8.7% year-over-year from $182.4 million in the same quarter last year to $198.3 million in this quarter. And finally, diluted net income per share increased from $0.27 in Q2/24 to $0.29 in Q2/25 – resulting in 7.4% year-over-year.

But while earnings per share grew only in the single digits, adjusted earnings per share increased 11.1% year-over-year to $0.30 and free cash flow increased even 23.2% year-over-year to $168.0 million.

Bigger Picture

When looking at the second-quarter results we see certainly solid numbers and good growth from Rollins. But we should never make the mistake and look only at one quarter, which is just a snapshot of the business. In case of Rollins, we can look at the quarterly revenue growth rates, and this is the first hint that underlines what a great business Rollins is. Of course, the quarterly revenue growth rate is fluctuating, but compared to many other companies these fluctuations are rather small and we also see growth rates accelerating in the last few years, which is a good sign.

And when looking not only at revenue, but also at the adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow conversion, we see high levels of stability since 2000. Not only is Rollins reporting more than 20 years of consecutive growth, but also has more than 75% of recurring revenue.

In the chart we also see the last two recessions and the industrial slowdown in 2015 and the chart is demonstrating that Rollins is not really affected. During the Piper Sandler 4th Annual Growth Frontiers Conferenc e, management made the following statement:

We've seen growth through just about every economic cycle, whether it be the great financial crisis back in 2008, 2009, the industrial slowdown in 2015 or more recently with COVID, we've continued to execute and successfully grow our business, continuing to see really good demand, very resilient business, an essential service with a significant amount of pricing opportunity.

During the same conference (and other conferences and presentations), management also talked about pricing and how it will contribute to growth in the years to come. Management also pointed out Rollins was able to increase prices in almost every market environment, which is another strong hint of a very stable business. During the above-mentioned conference, management also talked about pricing in excess of CPI:

And so we're continuing to focus on getting pricing that's in excess of CPI. So if CPI regresses back to a more 2% to -- a more historical trend of 2% to 3%, we really do believe that 3% to 4% is still a realistic goal for us as we think about the future. And that's helped our growth. If you go back the last 15 or 20 years prior to 2020, what you saw was growth that was maybe a 4% to 5% sort of growth profile. But more recently, what we've seen is 7% to 8% organic growth.

And management is confident it can continue to increase prices with a similar pace in the years to come. By the way, this is a strong hint for a wide economic moat around the business: If a company is able to raise prices constantly above inflation rates and not losing customers, it is a strong hint of a business that can “lock in” customers. Additionally, Rollins is an essential business, and management also sees the threat from do-it-yourself being rather limited:

Certainly, when you look at the business and you evaluate the pricing opportunities, a couple of things come to mind. One, this is very much an essential service. It's about protecting property. It's about protecting health and our consumers value it. We don't see a large amount of do-it-yourself for efforts in this space. People want to pay somebody to take care of this -- the pest and provide an environment that's conducive with their lifestyle. And so when you look at it, it's very essential. It's also in a really small ticket item, purchase item for our consumer. An annual pest control contract might be $500 or $600. It might range higher than that depending on the location you're in, and the pest you're treating, but generally, $500, $600. So when we look at it, small purchase item for our consumers, highly valued purchase item, very resilient. It's not something you can do once and forget about it. You've got to repeatedly treat the issue. And so when you look at this, what we've evaluated is the pricing opportunity and elasticity and the pricing, evaluating it through how our consumers value the service.

With a contract being worth about $500 annually, this results in about 0.1% of a typical property value (and in some regions and especially for high-end property even less). Rollins is therefore offering an essential service at a relatively low price with the potential damage risk is immense (in case of termites for example). Businesses offering services which only account for a fraction of overall costs and especially with the potential damage being immense are usually in a good position. During the JPMorgan U.S. All Stars 2025 Conference, management also underlined its strong brand names and customer trust:

So that presents us a pretty strong opportunity on the commercial side. And we have the power of the Orkin brand behind it. And what that means after being in business, Orkin next year will be -- have been in business for 125 years. And there's a lot of trust from consumers, both on the residential side and the commercial side that has been a lot of brand equity that has occurred over the last 125 years. And we feel like that puts us in a really strong position to continue to compete.

Combining all these points, we can certainly conclude that Rollins is a great business and most likely has a wide economic moat around its business. This is also visible in the chart, as Rollins has increased by more than 8,000% since the early 1990s while the S&P 500 increased about 1,800% in the same timeframe. And while Rollins actually lagged in the last 5 years, it still outperformed in the last 10 years.

In my last article I already pointed out the high return on invested capital Rollins reported in the last ten years. Only in one single year was the RoIC clearly below 20% and on average, the metric was 25.79% in the last ten years.

And Morgan Stanley mapped out the average RoIC of U.S. companies in the last six decades and with an RoIC close to 25%, Rollins is one of the best companies out there.

Continued Growth

And considering the high quality of the business as well as the wide economic moat, we can also assume that Rollins will continue to grow at a relatively high pace in the years to come. Analysts are estimating earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 9.94% in the next five years until fiscal 2029.

Looking at past growth rates, we can also see these numbers as reasonable. In the last ten years, revenue increased with a CAGR of 9.15% and earnings per share increased with a CAGR of 13.17%. Although the last ten years might not necessarily be representative for the business as we saw above average performance, revenue still increased with a CAGR of 7.14% since 2000 and adjusted EBITDA even increased by 14% annually in the same timeframe.

The company’s own growth algorithm is also expecting solid growth rates for the years to come. Revenue is expected to grow above-market due to solid organic growth as well as mergers and acquisitions. During the Piper conference, management made the following statement:

When we think about this business, we're a growth company. So what we like to provide our investors with is that steady double-digit rate of growth, which is 2/3 or 3/4 organic and 1/3 to 1/4 M&A, see tremendous opportunities to continue to provide that M&A growth and then turn that growth into double-digit earnings growth.

During the JPMorgan Conference, Rollins management also pointed out the positive impact that AI might have on the business. On the one side, its core business is threatened only very little by AI as pest control and similar services can’t be replaced by AI.
